2. Understanding Convexity Index

Nikhil Kaza

2026-07-21

Source: vignettes/b-understanding-convexity-index.qmd
Code
library(shapeindices)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(stringr)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 11))
theme_gallery <- theme_void(base_size = 10) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "bottom")

# defined here (quietly) so every inline `r CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI` /
# ci_auto() reference resolves regardless of where in the prose it first
# appears - re-shown, deliberately identical, in "The convexity index"
# section below for readers, since inline code is evaluated in document
# order and a chunk further down the file is too late for text that
# references it earlier
CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI <- 25
ci_auto <- function(poly, prep = NULL) {
  if (is.null(prep)) prep <- prepare_polygon(poly)
  n_tri <- if (is.null(prep$tri)) 0 else nrow(prep$tri)
  deterministic <- n_tri <= CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI
  # n_quad isn't passed explicitly - convexity_index()'s own default is
  # n_quad = 3, so the deterministic = TRUE branch here already gets the
  # quadrature refinement without asking for it
  res <- suppressWarnings(convexity_index(poly, deterministic = deterministic,
                                           n_lines = 5000, prep = prep))
  res$deterministic_used <- deterministic
  res
}
Code
square <- st_polygon(list(rbind(c(0,0), c(10,0), c(10,10), c(0,10), c(0,0))))

c_shape <- st_polygon(list(rbind(
  c(0,0), c(10,0), c(10,10), c(0,10), c(0,7), c(7,7),
  c(7,3), c(0,3), c(0,0))))

# turtle-graphics path: turn by a fixed angle each step, optionally growing
# the step length - the basis for the spiral below.
make_turtle_path <- function(n_steps, angle_deg, step0 = 1, step_growth = 0) {
  angle <- 0
  pos   <- c(0, 0)
  pts   <- matrix(pos, ncol = 2)
  step  <- step0
  for (i in seq_len(n_steps)) {
    angle <- angle + angle_deg * pi / 180
    pos   <- pos + step * c(cos(angle), sin(angle))
    pts   <- rbind(pts, pos)
    step  <- step + step_growth
  }
  st_linestring(pts)
}

# spiral: a turtle spiral (fixed turn angle, growing step length) buffered
# into a thick corridor - a single spiral notch instead of many small ones.
spiral <- st_buffer(
  make_turtle_path(n_steps = 48, angle_deg = 24, step0 = 0.35, step_growth = 0.09),
  dist = 0.35, endCapStyle = "FLAT", joinStyle = "MITRE", mitreLimit = 3
)

# maze: a proper grid maze (recursive-backtracker, like a Pac-Man board),
# rendered as blocky corridors - lots of turns, branches and dead ends,
# unlike the spiral's single winding path.
make_maze <- function(n_rows = 6, n_cols = 6, cell = 1, corridor = 0.6, seed = 1) {
  set.seed(seed)
  visited    <- matrix(FALSE, n_rows, n_cols)
  open_right <- matrix(FALSE, n_rows, n_cols)
  open_down  <- matrix(FALSE, n_rows, n_cols)

  # recursive backtracker: carve a spanning-tree maze by random depth-first
  # search, tracking the path on a stack so it can back up at dead ends
  stack <- matrix(c(1L, 1L), ncol = 2)
  visited[1, 1] <- TRUE
  while (nrow(stack) > 0) {
    r <- stack[nrow(stack), 1]; cc <- stack[nrow(stack), 2]
    cand <- list()
    if (r > 1      && !visited[r - 1, cc]) cand[[length(cand) + 1]] <- c(r - 1L, cc, 1L)
    if (r < n_rows && !visited[r + 1, cc]) cand[[length(cand) + 1]] <- c(r + 1L, cc, 2L)
    if (cc > 1     && !visited[r, cc - 1]) cand[[length(cand) + 1]] <- c(r, cc - 1L, 3L)
    if (cc < n_cols && !visited[r, cc + 1]) cand[[length(cand) + 1]] <- c(r, cc + 1L, 4L)
    if (length(cand) == 0) {
      stack <- stack[-nrow(stack), , drop = FALSE]   # dead end: backtrack
    } else {
      pick <- cand[[sample.int(length(cand), 1)]]
      nr <- pick[1]; nc <- pick[2]; dir <- pick[3]
      if (dir == 1) open_down[nr, cc] <- TRUE
      if (dir == 2) open_down[r, cc]  <- TRUE
      if (dir == 3) open_right[r, nc] <- TRUE
      if (dir == 4) open_right[r, cc] <- TRUE
      visited[nr, nc] <- TRUE
      stack <- rbind(stack, c(nr, nc))
    }
  }

  # render cells as squares and carved openings as connecting bridges, then
  # union into one blocky corridor polygon
  half <- corridor / 2
  make_square <- function(cx, cy) st_polygon(list(rbind(
    c(cx - half, cy - half), c(cx + half, cy - half),
    c(cx + half, cy + half), c(cx - half, cy + half), c(cx - half, cy - half)
  )))
  make_bridge <- function(cx1, cy1, cx2, cy2) {
    xr <- range(cx1, cx2); yr <- range(cy1, cy2)
    st_polygon(list(rbind(
      c(xr[1] - half, yr[1] - half), c(xr[2] + half, yr[1] - half),
      c(xr[2] + half, yr[2] + half), c(xr[1] - half, yr[2] + half),
      c(xr[1] - half, yr[1] - half)
    )))
  }

  pieces <- list()
  for (r in seq_len(n_rows)) for (cc in seq_len(n_cols)) {
    cx <- cc * cell; cy <- -r * cell
    pieces[[length(pieces) + 1]] <- make_square(cx, cy)
    if (open_right[r, cc]) pieces[[length(pieces) + 1]] <- make_bridge(cx, cy, cx + cell, cy)
    if (open_down[r, cc])  pieces[[length(pieces) + 1]] <- make_bridge(cx, cy, cx, cy - cell)
  }
  st_union(st_sfc(pieces))[[1]]
}
maze <- make_maze()

# combs: a base bar with several thin rectangular teeth - many deep,
# straight-sided notches rather than the stars' angled ones.
make_comb <- function(n_teeth = 6, base_height = 1, tooth_width = 0.6,
                       tooth_gap = 0.6, tooth_height = 4) {
  total_width <- n_teeth * tooth_width + (n_teeth - 1) * tooth_gap
  base <- st_polygon(list(rbind(
    c(0, 0), c(total_width, 0), c(total_width, base_height), c(0, base_height), c(0, 0)
  )))
  teeth <- lapply(seq_len(n_teeth) - 1, function(i) {
    x0 <- i * (tooth_width + tooth_gap)
    st_polygon(list(rbind(
      c(x0, base_height), c(x0 + tooth_width, base_height),
      c(x0 + tooth_width, base_height + tooth_height), c(x0, base_height + tooth_height),
      c(x0, base_height)
    )))
  })
  st_union(st_sfc(c(list(base), teeth)))[[1]]
}
comb <- make_comb()

make_star <- function(n_points, r_outer = 1, r_inner = 0.5, center = c(0, 0)) {
  n <- n_points * 2
  angles <- seq(pi/2, pi/2 + 2*pi, length.out = n + 1)[1:n]
  radii  <- rep(c(r_outer, r_inner), n_points)
  x <- center[1] + radii * cos(angles)
  y <- center[2] + radii * sin(angles)
  coords <- rbind(cbind(x, y), c(x[1], y[1]))
  st_polygon(list(coords))
}

n_seq     <- c(3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20)
stars_n   <- lapply(n_seq, make_star, r_outer = 1, r_inner = 0.5)
names(stars_n) <- sprintf("n = %d", n_seq)

ratio_seq <- c(0.9, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, 0.15, 0.05)
stars_r   <- lapply(ratio_seq, function(r) make_star(6, 1, r))
names(stars_r) <- sprintf("ratio = %.2f", ratio_seq)

# urchin: like make_star, but arms can be irregularly spaced (jitter_frac > 0)
# and/or have different lengths (r_outer as a vector, one entry per arm)
# rather than evenly spaced and equal - used to isolate symmetry from notch
# depth/count, since make_star's evenly-spaced, equal-length arms always
# have an exact opposite.
make_urchin <- function(n_arms, r_outer = 1, r_inner = 0.02, jitter_frac = 0, seed = 1) {
  set.seed(seed)
  n <- n_arms * 2
  base_angles <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = n + 1)[1:n]
  jitter <- if (jitter_frac > 0) runif(n, -jitter_frac, jitter_frac) * (2 * pi / n) else 0
  angles <- base_angles + jitter
  radii <- numeric(n)
  radii[seq(1, n, by = 2)] <- rep(r_outer, length.out = n_arms)
  radii[seq(2, n, by = 2)] <- r_inner
  x <- radii * cos(angles); y <- radii * sin(angles)
  coords <- rbind(cbind(x, y), c(x[1], y[1]))
  st_polygon(list(coords))
}

# even vs odd arm count, same (matched) notch depth, equal-length arms and no
# angular jitter - isolates the effect of an exact opposite arm existing or not
urchin_even <- make_urchin(8, r_inner = 0.03, jitter_frac = 0)
urchin_odd  <- make_urchin(7, r_inner = 0.03, jitter_frac = 0)

# irregular arm angles (no exact opposites at all) plus a much deeper notch -
# used later to show that symmetry, not notch depth, was the floor
urchin_asym <- make_urchin(17, r_inner = 0.004, jitter_frac = 0.35, seed = 3)

# evenly-spaced angles (like make_star) but a different length per arm - a
# distinct kind of asymmetry from urchin_asym's irregular angles
urchin_varylen <- make_urchin(7, r_outer = c(1, 0.55, 1.3, 0.7, 1.15, 0.6, 0.9), r_inner = 0.15)

# organic irregular polygon: random radius at each of a set of sorted angles
# around a centre - no repeating angular unit at all, structurally distinct
# from the whole star/urchin family
make_blob <- function(n_vertices = 12, seed = 1, roughness = 0.75) {
  set.seed(seed)
  angles <- sort(runif(n_vertices, 0, 2 * pi))
  radii  <- 1 + roughness * (runif(n_vertices) - 0.5) * 2
  x <- radii * cos(angles); y <- radii * sin(angles)
  coords <- rbind(cbind(x, y), c(x[1], y[1]))
  st_polygon(list(coords))
}
blob <- make_blob(12, seed = 3, roughness = 0.75)

star_demo <- make_star(6, 1, 0.3)   # used later in the diagnostic-figure gallery
hexagon_demo <- make_star(6, 1, 1)
# a family of shapes that are ALL convex, so any drop in convexity_index
# below 1 here would be a bug, not a feature - these isolate "shape"
# (elongation, vertex count) from "boundary complexity" (notches)
basic_shapes <- list(
  "square"             = square,
  "rectangle 10x2"     = st_polygon(list(rbind(c(0,0), c(10,0), c(10,2), c(0,2), c(0,0)))),
  "rectangle 20x1"     = st_polygon(list(rbind(c(0,0), c(20,0), c(20,1), c(0,1), c(0,0)))),
  "triangle"           = st_polygon(list(rbind(c(0,0), c(10,0), c(5,10), c(0,0)))),
  "regular hexagon"    = make_star(6, 1, 1),   # ratio = 1 -> no notch -> regular polygon
  "near-circle"        = st_buffer(st_sfc(st_point(c(0, 0))), dist = 5, nQuadSegs = 30)[[1]]
)

make_square_with_hole <- function(outer_half = 5, hole_frac = 0) {
  outer <- rbind(c(-outer_half, -outer_half), c(outer_half, -outer_half),
                 c(outer_half,  outer_half),  c(-outer_half,  outer_half),
                 c(-outer_half, -outer_half))
  if (hole_frac <= 0) return(st_polygon(list(outer)))
  hh <- outer_half * sqrt(hole_frac)   # so hole area / outer area == hole_frac
  hole <- rbind(c(-hh, -hh), c(-hh, hh), c(hh, hh), c(hh, -hh), c(-hh, -hh))
  st_polygon(list(outer, hole))
}

hole_fracs <- c(0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7)
holes      <- lapply(hole_fracs, function(hf) make_square_with_hole(hole_frac = hf))
names(holes) <- sprintf("hole = %.0f%%", 100 * hole_fracs)

hole_demo <- make_square_with_hole(5, 0.3)   # used later in the diagnostic-figure gallery

make_dispersed <- function(gap) {
  if (gap <= 0) {
    return(st_polygon(list(rbind(c(0,0), c(6,0), c(6,6), c(0,6), c(0,0)))))
  }
  r1 <- rbind(c(0, 0), c(3, 0), c(3, 6), c(0, 6), c(0, 0))
  r2 <- rbind(c(3 + gap, 0), c(6 + gap, 0), c(6 + gap, 6), c(3 + gap, 6), c(3 + gap, 0))
  st_multipolygon(list(list(r1), list(r2)))
}

gaps <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8)
disp <- lapply(gaps, make_dispersed)
names(disp) <- sprintf("gap = %.1f", gaps)

dispersed_demo <- make_dispersed(2)   # used later in the diagnostic-figure gallery

# the same (non-convex) shape at different absolute sizes, to check that
# convexity_index() is scale-invariant
size_factors <- c(1, 3, 10)
size_shapes  <- lapply(size_factors, function(f) c_shape * f)
names(size_shapes) <- sprintf("%gx", size_factors)

# rasterize any target polygon: keep every grid cell whose centre falls
# inside it, then union the cells - a faithful stand-in for what you get
# from vectorizing a raster/classification mask, without needing a raster
# package at all.
rasterize_polygon <- function(target, cell = 1) {
  target_sfc <- st_sfc(target)
  bb <- st_bbox(target_sfc)
  xs <- seq(bb["xmin"] + cell / 2, bb["xmax"], by = cell)
  ys <- seq(bb["ymin"] + cell / 2, bb["ymax"], by = cell)
  g   <- expand.grid(x = xs, y = ys)
  pts <- st_as_sf(g, coords = c("x", "y"))
  keep <- lengths(st_intersects(pts, target_sfc)) > 0
  g <- g[keep, , drop = FALSE]
  if (nrow(g) == 0) return(NULL)   # cell too big relative to target: no cell centres fall inside

  half <- cell / 2
  make_cell <- function(cx, cy) {
    st_polygon(list(rbind(
      c(cx - half, cy - half), c(cx + half, cy - half),
      c(cx + half, cy + half), c(cx - half, cy + half),
      c(cx - half, cy - half)
    )))
  }
  cells <- mapply(make_cell, g$x, g$y, SIMPLIFY = FALSE)
  st_union(st_sfc(cells))[[1]]
}

make_rotated_rect <- function(width = 14, height = 8, angle = 20, center = c(0, 0)) {
  ang <- angle * pi / 180
  corners <- rbind(c(-width/2, -height/2), c(width/2, -height/2),
                    c(width/2,  height/2), c(-width/2,  height/2),
                    c(-width/2, -height/2))
  rot <- matrix(c(cos(ang), sin(ang), -sin(ang), cos(ang)), 2, 2)
  corners <- sweep(corners %*% rot, 2, center, "+")
  st_polygon(list(corners))
}

# the three "true" (un-pixelated) shapes we'll rasterize at several
# resolutions: a smooth circle, a 6-point star, and a rectangle deliberately
# rotated relative to the pixel grid (an axis-aligned rectangle would
# rasterize with perfectly straight edges and defeat the point - real-world
# footprints are almost never grid-aligned either).
raster_targets <- list(
  circle    = st_buffer(st_sfc(st_point(c(0, 0))), dist = 8, nQuadSegs = 30)[[1]],
  star      = make_star(6, r_outer = 8, r_inner = 4),
  rectangle = make_rotated_rect(width = 14, height = 8, angle = 20)
)

# each true shape, rasterized at several different grid-cell sizes -
# "levels of pixellation" from coarse to fine. Chosen wide enough (4 down to
# 0.25, against shapes ~16 units across) to actually separate the coarse and
# fine ends - a narrower sweep (e.g. cell = 1 vs 0.5) barely moves the index
# at all, which is exactly the "pixelation doesn't seem to matter" trap this
# section exists to correct.
res_levels <- c(4, 1, 0.25)
raster_by_res <- lapply(raster_targets, function(target) {
  r <- lapply(res_levels, function(cell) rasterize_polygon(target, cell = cell))
  names(r) <- sprintf("cell = %s", res_levels)
  r
})

1 Introduction

This tutorial covers a shape index called convexity_index (CI). A polygon is convex when a line between any two interior points never crosses its boundary. CI turns that into a continuous measure: what fraction of such lines stay entirely inside?

In practice: build a constrained Delaunay triangulation (CDT), draw a line between every pair of triangle representative points, and take the area-weighted fraction that stays inside. A score of 1 means every line stayed inside; a single convex blob. The sections below show where the index gets this wrong and compare it to indices from the literature.

This construction - the probability that a random pair of interior points stays connected by a straight line - matches the convexity measure introduced by Zunic & Rosin (2004).1 Two comparison indices recur throughout this tutorial: moment_of_inertia_index (roundness, not convexity) and hull_ratio_index (a simpler, more boundary-sensitive convexity proxy) - both introduced in full below.

The rest of the tutorial works through how shape features affect CI: boundary complexity, overall shape, size, symmetry, holes, multi-part polygons, raster resolution. These are the properties that matter for choosing a shape indicator that holds up on real data.

2 Constrained Delaunay triangulation

“Delaunay triangulation” converts a set of points into well-shaped triangles, using the empty-circumcircle property to avoid long, sliver-shaped ones. Applied naively to a polygon’s vertices, it will happily fill in triangles across holes and gaps, since it only sees points, not edges.

A constrained Delaunay triangulation (CDT) instead treats the polygon’s edges and hole boundaries as constraints every triangle must respect. The triangulation covers exactly the polygon’s true interior, as shown below.

3 The convexity index

Definition. For a mass distribution with density ρ\rho and total mass W=∫PρW = \int_P \rho, let s,t∈Ps, t \in P be two independent points drawn from ρ/W\rho/W, and let f(s,t)∈[0,1]f(s,t) \in [0,1] be the fraction of the segment st¯\overline{st} lying outside PP - a purely geometric quantity fixed by PP’s own shape, independent of ρ\rho. (Here ss and tt are whole points of the polygon PP, not scalar coordinates.) Define

CI(ρ)=1−𝔼[f(s,t)]=1−1W2∬P×Pρ(s)ρ(t)f(s,t)dsdt\text{CI}(\rho) = 1 - \mathbb{E}[f(s,t)] = 1 - \frac{1}{W^2}\iint_{P \times P} \rho(s)\,\rho(t)\,f(s,t)\; ds\, dt

Unweighted, ρ\rho is just PP’s own indicator function and W=AW = A (area) - not a different definition, just ρ≡1\rho \equiv 1 on PP. CI=1\text{CI} = 1 exactly when PP is convex, since every interior segment then stays inside (f≡0f \equiv 0 on all of P×PP \times P). It falls as more of ρ(s)ρ(t)\rho(s)\rho(t)’s mass sits on point pairs with ff near 1.

Decomposition. Triangulate PP (constrained Delaunay triangulation, CDT) into triangles T1,…,TnT_1, \dots, T_n, and let ρ\rho be piecewise-constant, ρ≡ρi\rho \equiv \rho_i on TiT_i; write wi=ρi⋅area(Ti)w_i = \rho_i \cdot \text{area}(T_i) for that triangle’s own mass (wi=area(Ti)w_i = \text{area}(T_i) exactly, unweighted - convexity_index()’s own weight argument is literally this wiw_i). Splitting the double integral by triangle,

∬P×Pρ(s)ρ(t)f(s,t)dsdt=∑i=1n∑j=1nwiwjgij,gij=1area(Ti)area(Tj)∬Ti×Tjf(s,t)dsdt\iint_{P \times P} \rho(s)\rho(t)\,f(s,t)\,ds\,dt = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n w_i w_j\, g_{ij}, \qquad g_{ij} = \frac{1}{\text{area}(T_i)\,\text{area}(T_j)}\iint_{T_i \times T_j} f(s,t)\, ds\, dt

This is exact; no approximation yet, and no assumption about ρ\rho beyond piecewise-constant. Two facts about ff - not ρ\rho - simplify it further, also exactly. First, gii=0g_{ii} = 0: every TiT_i is convex, so a segment between two of its own points never leaves it, regardless of how ρ\rho weights TiT_i. Second, gij=gjig_{ij} = g_{ji}, since ff is symmetric in its arguments. The double sum over all n2n^2 ordered pairs therefore collapses to a sum over the (n2)\binom{n}{2} unordered pairs with i≠ji \ne j, each counted twice:

∑i,jwiwjgij=∑i≠jwiwjgij=2∑i<jwiwjgij\sum_{i,j} w_i w_j\, g_{ij} = \sum_{i \ne j} w_i w_j\, g_{ij} = 2\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j\, g_{ij}

Expanding W2=(∑iwi)2=∑iwi2+2∑i<jwiwjW^2 = \big(\sum_i w_i\big)^2 = \sum_i w_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j splits the denominator the same way, so, exactly,

CI(ρ)=1−∑i<jwiwjgij12∑iwi2+∑i<jwiwj=1−∑i<jwiwjgijW2/2\text{CI}(\rho) = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j\, g_{ij}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i^2 \;+\; \sum_{i<j} w_i w_j} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j\, g_{ij}}{W^2/2}

The 12∑iwi2\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i^2 term isn’t a correction bolted on afterward - it’s just the diagonal of W2=∬P×Pρ(s)ρ(t)dsdtW^2 = \iint_{P \times P} \rho(s)\rho(t)\,ds\,dt, falling out of the general formula automatically: gii=0g_{ii}=0 zeroes it out of the numerator, but it was never weighted by ff in the denominator to begin with. This is a cleaner situation than moment_of_inertia_index()’s or span_index()’s own weighted references, where the analogous same-piece term is a genuine positive quantity needing its own numerical treatment - here it vanishes from the numerator exactly, because ff itself is identically zero on the diagonal, not because some optimal-arrangement argument makes it negligible.

Estimation. The only approximation is in gijg_{ij} itself, and it never depends on ρ\rho or wiw_i at all - a double integral over Ti×TjT_i \times T_j with no closed form in general. convexity_index() estimates it as ĝij\hat g_{ij} by placing qq quadrature points in each triangle. The default, q=3q = 3, is a Hammer-Stroud rule at barycentric coordinates (2/3,1/6,1/6)(2/3, 1/6, 1/6) and its two permutations rather than q=1q = 1 at the centroid alone; it connects all q2q^2 point-pairs between TiT_i and TjT_j and averages the fraction of each segment’s length outside PP. Substituting ĝij\hat g_{ij} for gijg_{ij} gives the computed index

CÎ(ρ)=1−∑i<jwiwjĝijW2/2\widehat{\text{CI}}(\rho) = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j\, \hat g_{ij}}{W^2/2}

Larger qq makes ĝij\hat g_{ij} a better estimate of gijg_{ij}: q=1q=1 is a coarse stand-in for the integral (one point per triangle), q=3q=3 closes most of that gap, at roughly 9x the cost. convexity_index() defaults to n_quad = 3; drop to n_quad = 1 on meshes of more than a few hundred triangles, where the resulting O(9n2)O(9n^2) cost starts to matter (see “Does polygonization matter?” for measured numbers). The W2/2W^2/2 denominator itself is exact and unaffected by qq; it comes from gii=0g_{ii}=0 and gij=gjig_{ij}=g_{ji} above, not from any approximation, and cannot be disabled.

Deterministic vs. sampled. The procedure above is deterministic (a fixed quadrature grid, not literally exact - see convexity_index()’s own documentation for why it’s named deterministic, not exact) over all (n2)\binom{n}{2} triangle pairs, O(9n2)O(9n^2) with the default qq, which grows expensive fast on complex meshes. deterministic = FALSE instead estimates CI(ρ)\text{CI}(\rho) directly by Monte Carlo: draw n_lines independent pairs (s,t)∼ρ/W(s, t) \sim \rho/W (uniformly when unweighted, or from the per-triangle weighted density otherwise) and average f(s,t)f(s,t) - a direct, unbiased estimate of the defining integral above, with no triangulation or quadrature approximation at all. Checked against the deterministic method, the two agree within a couple of percentage points except at the deepest notches on the sparsest meshes.2 This tutorial switches to it automatically above 25 triangles, low enough that most “complicated” shapes below (maze, spiral, the finer rasterised meshes, the most irregular urchin) use it; only small meshes get the deterministic treatment.

CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI <- 25

#' Dispatcher for every CI number below: deterministic = TRUE
#' (convexity_index()'s own n_quad = 3 default applies automatically) for
#' meshes with CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI triangles or fewer,
#' deterministic = FALSE (5000 lines) above that. Adds `deterministic_used`
#' so callers can flag approximated values or draw the right kind of line
#' in a diagnostic plot.
ci_auto <- function(poly, prep = NULL) {
  if (is.null(prep)) prep <- prepare_polygon(poly)
  n_tri <- if (is.null(prep$tri)) 0 else nrow(prep$tri)
  deterministic <- n_tri <= CI_DETERMINISTIC_MAX_TRI
  res <- suppressWarnings(convexity_index(poly, deterministic = deterministic,
                                           n_lines = 5000, prep = prep))
  res$deterministic_used <- deterministic
  res
}

Two comparison indices recur throughout: moment_of_inertia_index (IMI, for Index of Moment of Inertia)3 and hull_ratio_index (HRI)4. IMI relies on triangulation; HRI doesn’t need it at all.

For the square, every line tested (its one triangle-pair, tested via 9 quadrature sub-lines) stays inside, so convexity is exactly 1. For the C-shape, the holed square, and the dispersed pair, several lines cut across a concavity and land outside, and the score reflects that.

The pixellated circle shown here (cell = 1, 62 triangles) is past this tutorial’s deterministic/random threshold, so its lines are a random sample rather than the exhaustive set. Some still cross the stairstep boundary, but because the index averages over area (or, for the random-line index, samples in proportion to it), those slivers barely move the score either way, and it stays close to 1.

The star is where the index starts to look unreliable. Visually it’s heavily non-convex, all spikes, but only a few of its quadrature-pair lines actually cross the boundary; most stay within the interior. The result is a score near 0.9: the convex middle dominates, and the spiky tendrils barely register.

Complicated shapes such as maze, comb, and spiral have smaller CI. Maze scores 0.683, comb scores 0.654, and spiral scores 0.298, all far below the star (maze and spiral via the deterministic = FALSE approximation; see “The convexity index” section above).

The key difference between the star and these shapes is the presence of a hub, a convex core that a lot of centroid lines pass straight through. Mazes, combs, and spirals have no such hub. That’s also why HRI for the star is very different from CI, but quite similar to CI for these other shapes.

4 Impact of Shape Parameters

4.1 Shape independent of boundary complexity

Everything in basic_shapes is convex: a square, two rectangles of very different elongation, a triangle, a regular hexagon, and a near-circle. CI is 1 for all of them, no matter how stretched, angular, or round:

CI measures concavity, not roundness or aspect ratio: the very thin rectangle 20x1 scores 1.000 on CI but only 0.095 on IMI, since IMI penalises elongation itself (area sitting far from the centroid). HRI, like CI, stays at 1 for all of these simple cases.

4.2 Size

CI is built entirely from ratios: fraction of line length outside the polygon, weighted by the product of triangle areas and normalised by the sum of those weights. Scaling a shape up leaves it unchanged, as shown below; a direct consequence of the construction, not an empirical finding. IMI and HRI are scale-invariant too. That matters for datasets with wide variation in polygon size: none of the three indices need extra normalisation.

4.3 Holes

CI falls steadily as the hole grows, from 1.000 with no hole to 0.410 once the hole covers 70% of the square’s area: more centroid-pairs on opposite sides of the hole have their connecting line pass over it. IMI declines too (to 0.169 at the largest hole), for a different reason: the remaining area sits farther from the centroid on average, and a ring is a less compact arrangement of area than a filled disk. HRI falls hardest of all, since the hole never touches the convex hull; its area is unaffected, so HRI tracks 1 - hole_frac exactly.

4.4 Multi-part (dispersed) polygons

For multi-part polygons, CI drops from 1.000 (touching, gap = 0) to 0.632 at the widest gap: every centroid-pair linking a triangle in one part to a triangle in the other now has to cross the empty space between them. IMI falls faster (to 0.169 at the same gap), since it weights area by squared distance from the centroid; pulling the parts apart doesn’t just send some lines outside, it pushes the whole shape’s area farther from centre, and that penalty compounds quadratically rather than linearly.

HRI drops to 0.429 at the same gap, close to CI’s 0.632, and nowhere near IMI’s 0.169. The convex hull simply grows to enclose the widening gap, so HRI and CI agree here even though they disagreed for holes.

4.5 Boundary Complexity

4.5.1 More points, same notch depth

Holding the inner/outer radius ratio fixed at 0.5 and increasing only the number of points (stars_n, built above) barely moves the index. That’s a real weakness, since visually these stars get spikier, not less so. HRI has no such blind spot: at n = 4 it reads 0.707, at n = 5 0.618, correctly flagging substantial non-convexity where CI still reports a near-perfect 0.99.

4.5.2 Same point count, deeper notches

Holding the number of points fixed at 6 and varying only notch depth (stars_r, built above), the index behaves as expected.

The index falls off smoothly and substantially, from 1.00 at ratio 0.9 to 0.50 at ratio 0.05, with none of the resolution artefact seen above, since a fixed 6-point star always triangulates into the same 10 triangles regardless of ratio. Comparing the two sweeps: the index tracks notch depth cleanly, but its sensitivity to notch count depends on how much convex interior is left to average over.

HRI falls much further over the same sweep, from 1.000 to 0.058 at the deepest notch, because hull_ratio_index counts every bit of the notch as lost area, with no averaging to soften it.

4.5.3 Raster resolution

Polygons digitised from raster data (land cover, imperviousness masks, building footprints from a classified image) inherit the raster grid as their boundary, so every edge is a stairstep at the pixel resolution. Below: a smooth circle, a 6-point star, and a rotated rectangle, each rasterised at three grid-cell sizes, coarse to fine.

CI is largely impervious to boundary pixellation, with one caveat. If the pixellation is coarse enough to erase real boundary detail (a star’s points blurring into a rectangle, say), the index for the resulting shape simply reflects that flatter shape. What matters is pixel size relative to the shape, not pixellation as such, worth remembering for small urban areas delineated by a coarse 1 km grid.

HRI is far more sensitive to the same stairstep noise, since it has no averaging or area-weighting to dampen the effect. At the coarsest cell size, the circle’s hull_ratio_index drops to 0.857 (true value 1.000) while CI barely moves (0.991); the rectangle shows the same pattern (0.737 vs. 0.952). The same pixellated geometry, in other words, can read as strongly non-convex or barely non-convex depending on which index you ask. IMI is less exposed to this kind of boundary noise too.

At the finest cell size, every mesh here has well over 25 triangles, so these are all computed with the deterministic = FALSE approximation rather than the exhaustive method: circle reads 1.000 (true, unpixelated circle 1.000), star reads 0.968, rectangle reads 1.000; all close to their coarser-mesh counterparts above, consistent with CI’s general insensitivity to pixellation holding up under the approximation too.

4.6 Symmetry

A real but modest effect, worth a mention rather than a deep dive. In a shape with an exact opposite (an even number of evenly-spaced arms, say), the line between two opposite arm-tips passes straight through the shared centre and stays inside: genuine interior, not an artefact. urchin_even and urchin_odd (8 vs 7 arms, matched notch depth) isolate it:

Losing that one exact-opposite pair costs 0.058 points of CI (0.370 vs 0.312, about 16% relative) - a real effect, but a modest one, partly because the same-piece diagonal term (see “The convexity index” above - a required part of the exact formula, not an optional correction) raises both values and compresses the gap between them. HRI, for comparison, barely reacts to parity at all (0.032 vs 0.033).

The other two panels show the same mechanism fading as regularity disappears. urchin_varylen keeps seven evenly-spaced arms but varies their length; no two arms alike, yet CI still runs 0.546 points above HRI, since angular spacing, not arm length, is what lets a line cut past a shorter arm. blob has no repeating angular unit at all, and that gap narrows to 0.234. Worth a check on manufactured or engineered shapes with genuine symmetry; a minor consideration for organic, real-world footprints.

5 Key takeaways

  • CI is 1 for any convex shape and falls as a shape gets more concave, holed, or spatially dispersed.
  • Sensitivity to boundary notches depends on the mesh being fine enough to sample across them; a coarsely-vertexed but visually spiky polygon can score misleadingly close to 1. Notch depth, by contrast, is picked up cleanly.
  • deterministic = TRUE (the default) is a fixed quadrature grid, not literally exact - that’s why the argument isn’t called exact. The default (n_quad = 3) samples 3 points per triangle instead of the centroid alone, closing most of the gap to the index’s true target at roughly 9x the cost, but a concavity small relative to triangle size can still slip between the fixed sample points. deterministic = FALSE (random-line Monte Carlo) converges to the true value as n_lines grows, and is what this tutorial uses above 25 triangles.
  • Symmetry has a real but modest effect: an exact-opposite pair of features gives the index one genuinely-interior line to lean on, worth a few points of CI (see “Symmetry” above). Worth a check on manufactured or engineered shapes; a minor consideration for organic footprints.
  • Overall shape (elongation, vertex count) and uniform scaling don’t move the index as long as it stays convex; a direct consequence of being built entirely from ratios.
  • Holes and multi-part dispersal both drag the index down, since piece-pair lines are forced to cross excluded or missing territory.
  • Raster resolution cuts both ways: too coarse manufactures fake stairstep non-convexity on straight edges, or erases real concave detail entirely. Know the cell size relative to the smallest real feature.
  • IMI agrees with CI on direction (both drop for holes and dispersal) but not magnitude, and it also responds to elongation, which CI ignores entirely.
  • HRI catches real non-convexity in low-point-count stars that CI’s mesh misses entirely, but it is far more sensitive to boundary digitisation errors.

None of the three indices is a strictly better version of the others. Each fails differently. So the right choice depends on which failure mode you can least afford.

On this page

Developed by Nikhil Kaza.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.1.
